directions.name: Restore the accepted-correct hot-band surface and trade paired c_shared scratch for a 3-stage A/B pipeline
directions.hypothesis: Round 11 falsified the half-CTA Pg2s idea very clearly: the hot-band kernel slowed from about 41.11 ms to 46.14 ms while barrier stalls rose from about 6.59% to 23.43% and mio throttle rose from about 0.32% to 9.20%. That says the issue is not that too many threads participate in staging; it is that the current two-stage hot-band pipeline still does not hide feed latency well once orchestration changes. The next Pg2s move should therefore be deeper overlap, not fewer issuers. For the fixed 256x128 hot-band path specifically, the repo can likely afford a 3-stage A/B ring if the epilogue falls back from paired c_shared export scratch to a single-stage export helper. That directly matches the user's stage and epilogue-budget idea: spend a little more epilogue time to buy another A/B prefetch stage on the dominant hot loop.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Tensor under-utilization from a too-shallow hot-band mainloop pipeline rather than pure DRAM bandwidth.
directions.risk: High. This changes both the hot-band mainloop schedule and the 64x64 export path, so correctness bugs can appear if the stage rotation or tail wait logic is wrong.
directions.stop_condition: Reject if correctness fails, if hot-band time does not improve over the restored surface, or if barrier/scoreboard remain flat after paying the extra shared-memory budget.

directions.name: Restore the accepted surface and try a light consumer-side B XOR/interleaved swizzle with no extra shared footprint
directions.hypothesis: The user explicitly called out bank-conflict fixes that stay on the consumer side and do not add a second CTA repack stage. The current hot-band kernel still uses a simple per-warp-group skew for B shared layout. That was enough to beat the older path, but the plateau near 38.6% tensor activity suggests operand delivery could still be underfeeding the tensor pipe. A light XOR or interleaved logical-column remap inside the existing B shared tile is the cleanest untried bank-conflict family because it changes lane-to-bank mapping without increasing barrier count, stage count, or shared-memory bytes.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Shared-memory bank behavior and operand delivery on the hot-band B consumer path.
directions.risk: High. A wrong logical remap will silently corrupt results, and even a correct remap can hurt global-to-shared coalescing enough to erase any bank benefit.
directions.stop_condition: Reject immediately if correctness fails or if the hot-band kernel time increases versus the restored accepted surface.

directions.name: Restore the accepted-correct implementation surface before any new experiment
directions.hypothesis: The round-11 subset-staging branch was decisively negative, and the current source now carries both the flat L2 swizzle and the harmful half-CTA Pg2s change. If the next higher-upside branch proves too invasive to land cleanly in one round, the safest move is to restore the accepted correct surface first and keep the search anchored on a known-good baseline rather than compounding experiments on top of a 31.76 ms regression.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Not a bottleneck attack; this is the reset path that preserves signal quality for later rounds.
directions.stop_condition: Use this if the higher-ranked directions do not build cleanly or if they fail correctness.

notes: Round 12/50 absorbs a strong negative result from the half-CTA Pg2s experiment. The hot-band kernel regressed badly, so the next staging-family move should deepen overlap by reallocating shared-memory budget instead of reducing the number of copy-issuing threads. The untried secondary family remains a consumer-side B swizzle that does not add CTA-level repacking.
